name: Miraikan, Tokyo
concept&appearance: Designed by Nikken Sekkei, the building embodies the concept of 'The house of science technology filled with light and wind.' The exterior features a sleek, curved glass facade that symbolizes transparency and openness to society. It uses transparent reinforced glass on the north/east sides to maximize natural light and heat-resistant semi-transparent glass on the west. The structure includes ten 'light towers' that pierce through the building to introduce natural light and ventilation. Located in the Tokyo Academic Park in the Odaiba district, the design integrates with the futuristic waterfront environment.
permanent_exhibitions: The permanent exhibitions are divided into three core zones: 1. 'Explore the frontiers' (5F): Explores the universe, Earth, and life on various scales. Key exhibits include the 'Neutrino' observation model, a walk-through model of the 'International Space Station' (ISS), and the 'LE-7A Rocket Engine'. It questions our place in the universe and the future of the Earth's environment. 2. 'Create your future' (3F): Focuses on future society and lifestyles driven by technology. Key exhibits include 'Hello! Robots' (featuring various robots), androids like 'Otonaroid', and the 'Internet Physical Model' which visualizes data flow. It invites visitors to think about how to live with technology. 3. 'Discover your Earth': Centered around the Geo-Cosmos, this zone connects the other areas and allows visitors to experience the Earth's dynamic changes through scientific data.
central_hall: Known as the 'Symbol Zone', the central hall is a vast open atrium that spans from the 1st to the 6th floor, creating a dynamic vertical space filled with natural light. The centerpiece is the 'Geo-Cosmos', a 6-meter diameter spherical display suspended in the air that realistically projects the Earth's shifting appearance using over 10,000 OLED panels (upgraded to latest LED technology) with a resolution exceeding 10 million pixels.
special_effects_theaters: Dome Theater GAIA: A hemispherical dome theater located on the 6th floor with a screen diameter of 15.24 meters and a 23-degree tilt. It features a 3D full-dome video system powered by two Christie 4K RGB pure laser projectors and an 8.1-channel sound system. It screens planetarium programs and 3D science films like 'The Man from the 9 Dimensions' to provide an immersive experience of the universe.
science_popularization_activities: The museum employs 'Science Communicators' who explain exhibits and facilitate dialogue between the public and scientists. Activities include daily science shows, experimental workshops in the 'Co-Studio', and 'Curiosity Field' for children. It offers specific school partnership programs such as the 'Encounter with Earth' learning resource and the SDGs workshop 'Steer Towards the Future!', as well as the 'Picture Happiness on Earth Project' involving teenagers in content creation for the Geo-Cosmos.
